speaker: interviewer
text: --Irene Gut-Opdyke-- and this is tape 7. Irene, you just escaped from the German major. Where did you go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I joined the partisans because before I left [NON-ENGLISH], he said that he has a cousin that is, you know, with partisans and a way-- when I go, I should escape and go to the partisans. So because I was-- I didn't want to go with the Germans, you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: So that's what I did. I joined the partisans. And I even, for the first time in my life, I fell in love with Janek and-- Riddel, his name-- Janek Riddel. And his-- he was the only one son. And the mother was so happy, she prepared-- preparing wedding that was supposed to be in the forest.

speaker: interviewee
text: But just before-- day before the wedding, there was a little action that Janek said they needed guns. And small group of German were going through the village. And he never come back. He was killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you know how or--

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was fighting. They were-- the partisans were trying to attack that German thing. And the German was stronger than they counted. And men come and say, Mrs. Riddel, your son fell.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so I become widow before I become bride. I was so upset. I blamed God again. Why me?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I put-- pour myself with all my heart and soul into the partisans. And I spoke German, and I spoke Russian. I didn't carry gun. But I was a messenger girl from one place. I was trying-- inch closer to my home. 1945, Russia took over Poland. We won the war, lost the freedom.

speaker: interviewer
text: Irene, the forest that you were a partisan, was that the same forest where your friends were hiding?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, no. That wasn't-- on the way from Ukraine through Poland, middle of Poland, you know, and all the way almost towards the Russian-- towards the German border now we go. That's completely different areas, different parts. But it's still a Russian pursuing the German retreating now, you know? And we are right in-between.

speaker: interviewer
text: Had you had no connection to friends--

speaker: interviewee
text: Not-- I didn't have a connection with family or anybody else. So now I am-- it is 1945. And I am very close to my home where my parents live. I don't know if I, you know, find them. There was close-- and I stopped at little, little temple, you know, not regular but just-- and I went to the-- to the rabbi there and tell him my name and ask him if he knows of the people that I saved, mentioned some name. And he said oh, yes. A couple with a little baby, with a boy, they are leaving to go-- to go. But they live in Ryczywol. That is very close now to the place I lived before.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: That is now--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is this already--

speaker: interviewee
text: Now is the--

speaker: interviewer
text: --after the war?

speaker: interviewee
text: --after-- after the war, yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you--

speaker: interviewer
text: Where were you when the war ended?

speaker: interviewee
text: I was on the way going to, to see my parents. So I was trying to get to my home then.

speaker: interviewer
text: Still with the partisans?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, that-- partisans now was sort of resolved. I am now alone trying to reach my home. And I will now-- I stop at the temple trying to find out. And the rabbi did go out and was showing to me that the Heller family live here and here. They are on their way to, to, to leave. I was going to them when I was arrested now by the-- by the Russians, by the NKVD because of my association with the partisans. Because we were fighting the German and Russians. And Russians came as the saviors, you know. They come as the saviors to get to go back-- and, you know.

speaker: interviewee
text: So they put out orders that everybody have to go to lay your gun. Well, I did not even know. But they arrested me. I didn't know what to do. I didn't know where I find my parents-- if I find them.

speaker: interviewee
text: And about ten days later, I didn't know that anybody knew that I am arrested. In the morning, opportunity arrive. The window was open, the bars far apart. I was very skinny. I was five minute with the open windows.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was about half a floor, you know? Not-- not two floors, but floor and a half tall. And I jumped. Who left the window open?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, the window was open for air, I guess. And the bars were apart. But there was no jail yet.

speaker: interviewee
text: There was a-- before they put you in jail, they, you know-- what you call it? They ask you question and this. So they woke me up day and night, asking me question. I didn't know what to tell them.

speaker: interviewee
text: I was with a open window just for five minutes. Without thinking, I jumped. Good the ground was-- I fell over the fence and the ground was soft. I fell like a cat on all four. Then my legs were just like rubber. But I was free.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I go, I go to Heller. That's what the rabbi told me. So I go there. I ring the bell. Russian soldier came out. That was Heller. He looked at me. He said, are you Irene? I said, yes. He say, look, I am a-- Heller's brother. He put Russian uniform fighting the Germans. So now Ida came down. They could not keep me because they knew that I was arrested. The rabbi noticed and told them. And they were talking with the commissar. But I am now here. I could not go back. So Heller's brother in the Russian uniform took me to Cracow. And in Cracow, I was in Dr. Liwszyc, you know, home with my feet up because I broke some vein in my-- in my feet. And then I was wanted in my home now because the Russian were so mad that I escaped that they make me big stink with-- in partisans. So now I was in a, a [NON-ENGLISH], a home a little, from one to another. I was like a mother leaving, you know? And I am wanted. So now I could not find my family. They find out that my father was killed. Just before Russia took over, the German killed my father. Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: He wore the band as a Pole, you know. And they took him back, you know, to the-- to work with his factory and this. And I guess two drunken German soldiers told him to go on the street. He must look wrong at him. They took gun and shot him. And now my mother is so scared. She did not know what it will be. So she picked up the three sisters that was with her, moved someplace else, took another name. There is no way I could find anyone. And I am wanted.

speaker: interviewee
text: So my sister-- my-- the friends we met in Cracow-- that is the big table that they sit in, you know, in the pictures? And, and they, they-- and Franka wroted my story, you know, wroted my story. And they smuggle me out to Germany to Jewish DP camp, Hessisch-Lichtenau. Tell me how they smuggled you out.

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they made me a false paper. I made my hair black, you know. And that's how-- because the Russian were looking now for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I came to Hessisch-Lichtenau and naturally, I took my name back. I talked to the rabbi and to this-- and I was now so excited to see young men and women, Jewish children. I was in a Jewish camp now. They were already with Haganah fighting for Israel. We're rushing it a little bit. Lichtenau was where?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Germany?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Germany, in Bavaria.

speaker: interviewer
text: And you were in a DP camp? A--

speaker: interviewee
text: That was a--

speaker: interviewer
text: --Jewish DP camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: There was the DP camp. I was-- I, I was-- they helped me to escape because otherwise, they would arrest me. They would put me probably in some Russia, in some thing. So I am there now. And my hair start growing out rainbow color, from one to another. Well, the young Jewish boys and girls that were going now-- going with Haganah, fight for Israel. I could not find my home. This is my home now. So I start learning Hebrew. I wanted go. But I become very sick, landed for two months in a German hospital. I did have a, a, a enlarged heart, one thing. And I have diphtheria, which is children's disease. And I was fainting. I could not even lift my head. I was very sick.

speaker: interviewee
text: So when I returned about two months later, they left, the young people. And they left me with the old people and children. I didn't know what will be with my life. I came there 1946.

speaker: interviewee
text: 1948-- now group of men come from United Nation, from League of Nations. They came just to see the camp. And one of them was American. They want me to be interviewed by him. I spoke Polish, German, Russian, and Yiddish. And he spoke English and French-- six languages, not one common.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did the Jewish people know who you were?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh yes. As soon as I-- I came there-- but I soon told them I-- you know, and what I did, you know. They were very proud of me. That's the reason they want me to be interviewed by the man, you know. But I didn't speak English, so there was-- somebody translated my story. And the man said-- there was no romance-- he said, America will be very proud to have you. 1949, I came to this country, to New York. And I was alone, without money, family, marketable skills, not one word of English. But one thing I did have-- I was free. And America did not owe me anything. I owed America. She adopted me.

speaker: interviewee
text: So second day in United States, I find myself working at garment center, vestment foundation. And I worked there for five-- worked it for five years. And now I become United States citizen. I pledge allegiance to America, to the land of the free.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, near the United Nation building, I stop at little cafeteria. The place was full. And men come-- there was one chair empty. He looked at me. He say, I know you from some place. He start to read me my story. And he was a widower, invite me to dinner. Six weeks we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was his name?

speaker: interviewee
text: Bill-- William K. Opdyke. Bill Opdyke-- handsome, wonderful man.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was he doing in New York?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, he was with the-- he was with United Nations, you know. And so he did find me. And when he married me-- six weeks later. And we went to Arizona, where our little girl was born. Year to year, God give me a-- give us child. My husband was not young. And I-- what I went through, God gave me family. I did have now family on my own. [CRYING] And I put the biggest sign on my memory-- do not disturb! I didn't want to know about war, Holocaust prosecution. I wanted to know America. And I wanted raise my child. And she grew-- become beautiful young girl. At 17, she find a mate and married. And my husband become not well. He become sick. One day, I was reading newspaper. The newspaper said Holocaust never happened. It is propaganda. Well, that put me on fire. How anybody can say so-- I was very-- I was there. So I was yelling and screaming. My husband belonged to the Rotary Club in Yorba Linda. They invite me to speak for the first time in 1980. I told my story to the Rotarians. There was a rabbi, Rabbi Asa from Temple Beth Tikvah in Fullerton. He sent my story to Jerusalem. First, he invite me to speak in a temple to the young people. He sent my story to Jerusalem. From Jerusalem, we received four letters from people I saved. There was a-- the survivors uniting. First letter I received from the young man whose life began in German major villa. He called me mother.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who were these four?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were letter from, from Hellers. And there were letter from Heshu. And there were letter from-- I have the letter. From four people, they, they-- my story was proven. And, in 1982, I was invited to Jerusalem to plant a tree on the Avenue of the Righteous.

speaker: interviewee
text: And there I prayed for Israel. Because if I would go with Haganah, my life would be completely different. I would be long time Jewish and-- no, I was heartbroken. Why I couldn't? I wanted go with Haganah. I could not find my family.

speaker: interviewee
text: But now, I am there. And I made my vow that, for rest of my life, I would spend to try to bring people together, regardless of nationalities, religion, color, or creed.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you feel at that moment when they-- when they said, here is the woman who's a Righteous Gentile?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I did not know that I am heroine. I thought that everybody has the-- I mean, I wanted to help. I was not worried I'm heroine or no. They told me here that I am heroine. But I don't-- I just wanted to-- I survived. And it's my responsibility to tell this story. And I did not know that I can speak like that. I traveled all around United States, in Canada, Poland, Germany, whoever invites me-- speak to Rotary Clubs, Kiwanis Clubs, churches, temples. Now I even-- two months ago, I, I will show you downstairs-- Irene's Museum, my, my-- I get many, many honors from the Jewish community. Two months ago, I brought that-- the Monsignor from the Catholic Church here into Jewish temple in, in Irvine. He brought it a blessing for me from Pope. I received blessing from Pope because I saved many human lives.

speaker: interviewer
text: Which Monsignor was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Father Carp here from the John, John Paul II church here. The Catholic-- the whole community, not only agree-- the whole community, some of the, the Catholics never been in a temple. It was unbelievable to see that Monsignor in the red outfit kissing the rabbi and the rabbi kissing the Monsignor, and the people, each other, Jews and Christian, hugging and kissing. It was like one big family.

speaker: interviewer
text: Who was the rabbi?

speaker: interviewee
text: Rabbi, Rabbi King in Irvine, Temple Shir Ha-Ma'alot-- I cannot pronounce.

speaker: interviewer
text: Where is your daughter now, Irene?

speaker: interviewee
text: My daughter lives in Portland, Oregon. And I-- many times when I speak that direction, you know, I see her.

speaker: interviewer
text: How does she feel about your--

speaker: interviewee
text: She's very proud. My two grandsons are so proud of me. I mean, you know, my daughter has the most wonderful-- now I go for Thanksgiving. She sended me tickets to go. She already made that appointment for me to speak in a church. She did not even ask me. She knew that this is a-- children that went to drugs and, you know, that are lost children. And she knew that, that-- the priest was asking if I would speak. And I go and speak. And in schools-- I spoke to all-black school, very poor school in a-- in a dilapidated-- in the towns. I am not afraid. I have a 300, 400, 500 kids. When I finish, they cry. They stay in long line for hug and kisses. When I speak in a school, many times one of them-- some of them say, thank you for my people. Some say, look, I am Polish, and I am proud of you.

speaker: interviewee
text: I have a children that told me, Mrs. Opdyke, please, please forgive me. I say, for what? I am German. And I am so ashamed. I hug him. I say, honey, you're not guilty. You were not born. So they ask me, what should I do? I say, did you hurt me? Because you're German, then you're Nazi. The skinheads, they will come to you and tell you to join them. Tell them where to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: I say, you are the future leaders. You have to stand against the new Nazi, the skinheads, the Ku Klux Klans. They are nothing. They are losers, and you are not. You must do that. And when I finish, they don't run. They stay in line. I know beyond shadow of the doubt that I make the difference. And it's not easy for me. I am 72-years-old woman.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you find your family finally?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I finded my family through when the film was made, that Elie Wiesel and Sister Rittner wrote it. And I was sent to Poland. I find that one sister. And she knew where the other were.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year was this?

speaker: interviewer
text: A year ago?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1984, excuse me.

speaker: interviewer
text: What happened to your mother?

speaker: interviewee
text: My mother died in 1957, which I did not know. And my--

speaker: interviewer
text: Died of what?

speaker: interviewee
text: She did have stroke.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other sister?

speaker: interviewee
text: And the other-- the-- I have all four sisters. We were five. And I have-- we are all five, yes. But one of the sisters that was with me in the-- in the-- she's very sick. She's very sick. She cannot travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see her?

speaker: interviewee
text: And Marushka, too, cannot travel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you see them?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. I've been three times now in Poland. Matter of fact, I spoke in Poland. My sister, [? Bronia, ?] she writes beautiful poems. She's-- and she's the president of senior club. So she invited people my age and older. For the first time, I spoke there in Polish, which was very hard for me because my husband was American. I didn't speak Polish.

speaker: interviewer
text: Let me ask you something. You told me that you were raised not knowing that there is anti-Semitism in Poland. When you went back to Poland now, did you see that there is anti-Semitism?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I, I spoke-- my sister know. They're very proud, all sister. I never-- I went back to the-- to the-- you know, to see-- to Auschwitz and this. I was there. And I was so mad. Carloads of German children were coming. And they were very happy. I felt like to go and punch them in the nose. Now, I will not say that there are not some people anti-Semitism, but there are people that-- like the-- from schools, children, and this, that they have the scouts, they're staying every day by the wall where the killing was. And the flowers are brought. There are people that cry. And there are people that are like in every country. But I don't think that Poland really deserved the name that it get-- did get. They did not. Because first of all, Poland was a small country that have the biggest concentration of Jewish people. And Hitler wanted Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he-- Poland could not say, don't build the crematoriums. The people are not free. They were enslaved, too. And so, he brought it to Poland.

speaker: interviewee
text: From all the countries, he builded the crematoriums and build it from all countries people there. To be able to see how many people helped would have to be every Polish man, woman, and child to save someone. And that's not possible.

speaker: interviewer
text: You're, you're absolutely right. And we do know that there were a lot of Polish people who did help. And that's quite evident. But on the other hand, there was a pogrom in one of the small towns. And I'm sure you know--

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: --you know where it was--

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you--

speaker: interviewer
text: --after the war.

speaker: interviewee
text: I tell you what happened. There were so-called Poles-- like when the war started, the German put their-- put their-- you know, the, the criminals in a Polish uniforms and attack. And they say Poland attack Germany. That's the-- done. Now, one side-- on one side were the Ukrainian. And the other side, you did have a, a German that spoke Polish, that when the German just wiggled that they're coming, they-- because my father was persecuted by them because we came from middle of Poland to, to, to Oberschlesien.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we were the Poles. We were the persecuted. So some of them-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I would not say that there maybe not were Polish people involved. But they are sort of so-called people-- Polish people. I know that.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I am, you know, surprised the hate is so great. Like, I was just now-- someplace I heard that Polish lumber builded the Auschwitz, that Polish worker did building. What, the German were supposed to send the-- I mean, that's the silliest thing.

speaker: interviewer
text: And Polish worker? They were slaves. My sisters were forced to work. They were not, not, not paid. So, you know-- and another thing, right in the beginning, the newspaper said, Polish concentration camps, Polish death camps. They were not Polish. They were German-built concentration camp in Poland. But the name came out. And the rescuers came too late to focus.

speaker: interviewee
text: [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] There is two, two parts to this story. The people that were saved by Christian, they came too late to focus. And by those people that were in concentration camp, they say they did not suffer.

speaker: interviewer
text: We'll stop for a moment.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And then we'll talk about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes, I am through now with my story.
